_comment: Absolute performance budgets — the single source of truth for all three perf scopes (DB-layer runner, k6 HTTP, BenchmarkDotNet). A scope exits non-zero when a measured value breaches its budget. Most budgets are calibrated from a 1M-row run on a dev machine (2026-06); RECALIBRATE on your target/CI hardware (set each ~20-30% above the observed p95/mean). A missing key = measure but never fail.
_comment_stats: The statistics aggregates (statsSummary/statsLatencyPercentiles/statsTokenUsage/statsModelBreakdown/statsCostEstimate/statsCallTrends) translate to server-side GROUP BY/sum/percentile_cont — they do NOT client-evaluate. Their cost is one scan of the windowed rows: sub-second at 1M after planner statistics are current. The ~3.7-4.4s seen on the first 1M run (issue #246) was a stale-statistics artifact — a freshly bulk-seeded table with no ANALYZE made the planner pick a nested-loop plan that random-read the whole table; the same SQL runs ~270-880ms once analyzed (3452ms -> 478ms on EXPLAIN). The seeder now ANALYZEs after the bulk load and migration TuneAgentCallAutovacuum keeps stats fresh in production, so these budgets are real measured p95 + ~30-45% headroom on a 1M dev run (2026-06). The three heaviest (latencyPercentiles ~880ms full sort, tokenUsage/callTrends ~780-880ms bucketed full scans) are scan-bound — no index helps a full-window aggregate; sub-second at 10M+ would need pre-aggregated rollups. RECALIBRATE on your CI hardware (~20-30% above observed p95). statsPulse is the exception to the scan-bound rule: a trailing-60-minute window on indexed CreatedAt resolves to an index range scan (~1.6ms p95 on a fresh 1M seed). Its budget is NOT plain p95+headroom: every db-layer run's ingest probe drops 5k freshly-timestamped rows into that trailing hour, so on kept-DB iterate runs the measurement climbs ~0.7ms per 1k accumulated probe rows (observed 1.6 -> 5.2 -> 8.1 -> 12.5 -> 19.5ms over five back-to-back runs (re-verification ~19.1ms)) until rows age out after 60min. The 25ms budget covers the canonical fresh-seed run plus ~5-6 iterate runs within an hour while staying far below the regression signature (losing the CreatedAt index means a full scan, 250ms+). A statsPulse breach after a deep iterate loop = re-seed before chasing a regression.
_comment_anomaly: anomalyTimeline guards the anomaly-dashboard timeline aggregate (GetAnomalyCountsByAgentAsync): a GROUP BY (bucket, agent) with bitmask split counts over WHERE OutlierFlags <> 0, which rides the partial outlier index — the seeder flags ~3% of calls, so at 1M rows the scan touches ~30k rows, far cheaper than the full-window stats aggregates. Recalibrated 2026-07-03 after the traces-sorting columns widened the row (TotalTokens/CacheHitRate): observed p95 54.8ms warm, 64.8ms on the first cold pass after a fresh seed — the budget covers the cold pass.
_comment_traceSort: agentCallsListSort* guard the traces table's server-side column sorts (SortBy whitelist) measured at their worst case: no narrowing filter, whole-table top-50 at 1M rows. The nullable columns (LatencyMs/TotalTokens/CacheHitRate) sort with a '(col IS NULL)' pre-key for nulls-last-in-both-directions, which the planner cannot serve from the plain single-column index — the plan is a parallel seq scan + top-N heapsort (~230ms of the total; the rest is the page COUNT). ToolCount rides its index via incremental sort but its low cardinality makes that no cheaper. Filtered sorts (project/agent/time window — the normal UI state) are far cheaper. If these ever blow past budget, the lever is a raw-SQL '(col DESC NULLS LAST, Id DESC)' index pair per column plus NULLS LAST ordering EF can't express — deliberately not paid while worst case sits ~300-440ms. agentCallsListByToolName guards the ToolName EXISTS semi-join against AgentCallToolEntity ((ToolName, AgentCallId) index); agentCallToolNames guards the project-scoped DISTINCT picker ((ProjectId, ToolName) index-only scan). Calibrated 2026-07-03 at 1M calls / ~600k tool rows: sorts p95 310-440ms, byToolName ~297ms, toolNames ~72ms.
_comment_testRunStats: testRunStatsBySuite/testRunStatsBySuitePage guard the suite-scoped TestRunStats projection query (issue #253). The test-suites controller previously materialized the whole TestRunStatsEntity table and filtered in memory on every suites list + single-suite GET; the fix pushes the suite set into SQL (WHERE SuiteId IN (...)), backed by the existing index on TestRunStatsEntity.SuiteId. The query returns only the rows for the requested suite(s), so it is an index range read independent of total table size. testRunStatsPassTotals/testRunStatsRecentCohorts guard the dashboard's server-side TestRunStats aggregates (issue #288): the pass-rate totals (single scalar GROUP BY row) and the sparkline cohorts (GROUP BY (GroupId, EndpointId) ordered by max(RunCompletedAt), capped at 50) that replaced two O(all-history) materializations in DashboardStatistics. All four budgets are UNCALIBRATED PLACEHOLDERS (set conservatively; the aggregates scan the ~25k-row seeded table but return a bounded result) — RECALIBRATE on a full ~1M / 25k-test-run seed (set ~20-30% above the observed p95).
